text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY — SURVEILLANCE VISIT NOTE
2-Year Post-Treatment Survivorship Assessment

ATTENDING: [Head and Neck Oncology]
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, surveillance follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine 2-year post-treatment surveillance and survivorship visit. Patient is a 54-year-old never-smoking Taiwanese-American female presenting for scheduled follow-up after definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Chen is a 54-year-old never-smoking female with a history of HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, staged at T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition, stage III), initially diagnosed approximately 2 years ago. She was treated with definitive concurrent IMRT (70 Gy in 35 fractions, simultaneous integrated boost technique) with weekly cisplatin (40 mg/m2 x 6 of 7 planned cycles; one cycle held for transient creatinine elevation, cumulative dose approximately 390 mg/m2). She completed treatment without hospitalization during the active treatment phase, though she did require a brief 3-day admission approximately 2 weeks post-treatment completion for pain management and IV hydration in the setting of grade 3 mucositis and dehydration. She subsequently had an uncomplicated post-treatment course. Post-treatment PET-CT and MRI neck at 12 weeks confirmed complete metabolic and radiographic response. PEG tube was removed approximately 5 months post-treatment after oral intake recovered. She has been followed routinely with serial imaging and clinical exams, all of which have been reassuring.

Today she presents for her 2-year surveillance visit. She reports feeling very well overall. She has returned to her full-time role as a high school principal, which she finds deeply rewarding. She notes that her energy level is back to what she considers her baseline. She continues to have mild dry mouth, which she describes as a minor annoyance that she has largely adapted to. Taste remains somewhat different from before treatment — she says, 'food tastes about 80% normal at this point; things are a little blander than before, but I'm not complaining.' She denies any new throat pain, odynophagia, neck masses, ear pain, otalgia referred to the ear, hemoptysis, or unintentional weight loss. No hoarseness or voice change. Jaw opening has improved significantly compared to earlier in her post-treatment course and she is no longer doing formal exercises. She denies cough, dyspnea, or pleuritic chest pain. No constitutional symptoms.

Current oncologic concern: None active at this visit. She denies any new or worsening symptoms that would be concerning for recurrence.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
(+) Mild xerostomia, chronic and stable, managed with pilocarpine and oral hydration
(+) Mild residual dysgeusia — partial taste recovery, approximately 80% of pre-treatment baseline per patient's own estimate
(+) Occasional mild dysphagia with very dry or chunky foods; no aspiration symptoms; tolerating regular diet
(+) Mild fatigue on days with very heavy workload — patient attributes this to professional demands, not cancer or treatment-related
(+) Cold intolerance improving on current levothyroxine dose
(-) No new neck pain, throat pain, or odynophagia
(-) No hemoptysis or hematemesis
(-) No otalgia, ear pain, or hearing change since last visit
(-) No diplopia, facial numbness, or cranial nerve symptoms
(-) No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis
(-) No weight loss; weight is stable
(-) No constipation or bowel habit changes (hypothyroidism now well-managed)
(-) No chest pain or palpitations
(-) No new skin changes, rashes, or lymphadenopathy perceived by patient

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress, appears her stated age, well-nourished and well-groomed. Pleasant and engaged.
Vitals: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 36.8°C, Weight 61.1 kg (up from 59.5 kg at PEG removal, near pre-treatment baseline of 62 kg).
Head/Face: No facial asymmetry. No palpable masses in the scalp or face. Temporomandibular joints non-tender; jaw opens symmetrically to approximately 41 mm (essentially at pre-treatment baseline of 42 mm; resolved trismus).
Oral cavity/Oropharynx: Oral mucosa mildly dry consistent with known xerostomia; no mucosal ulceration, leukoplakia, or erythematous patches. Residual hyperpigmentation of the bilateral neck skin (radiation field) — stable, macular, non-tender. Oropharynx: Post-radiation mucosal changes at the base of tongue — mild mucosal thickening without discrete mass, ulceration, induration, or abnormal vascularity. No exophytic or endophytic lesion identified on direct inspection. Tonsils absent (Note: tonsillar region appears post-radiation).
Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy — no firm, fixed, or matted nodes in levels I-V bilaterally. Left level IIA region: soft, no discrete nodularity. Mild bilateral radiation-related fibrotic firmness of the anterior neck soft tissues — stable and unchanged from prior visits. A faint carotid bruit is audible on the left, stable from prior examination — patient is under carotid duplex surveillance by vascular surgery.
Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed in clinic): Nasopharynx clear, no masses. Bilateral nasal passages patent. Base of tongue: Post-radiation mucosal appearance; mucosa mildly irregular in texture consistent with known radiation changes; no discrete lesion, ulcer, or mass. Vallecula patent. Epiglottis: Normal appearance and mobility. Arytenoids: Symmetric, mobile bilaterally. True vocal cords: Mobile bilaterally with normal adduction and abduction, no lesion or irregularity. Subglottis clear. Piriform sinuses: Symmetric, no pooling or irregularity. Overall appearance: Consistent with stable post-radiation changes; no evidence of mucosal recurrence.
Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. PEG site healed without complication — well-healed, flat scar.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. CN II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits.

---

LAB RESULTS
TSH: 1.7 mIU/L (normal; well-controlled on current levothyroxine dose)
Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (within normal limits)
CBC: WBC 6.4 x10^9/L, Hgb 13.1 g/dL, Plt 218 x10^9/L — normal
BMP: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cr 0.72 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL — normal
Magnesium: 2.0 mEq/L (normal; no longer supplementing)
LFTs: Within normal limits

---

IMAGING RESULTS
MRI Neck with Contrast (2-year surveillance, reviewed today): No evidence of locoregional recurrence. Post-treatment mucosal changes at the left base of tongue are stable compared to the 12-month MRI. No suspicious enhancement, no new mass, no ulceration. The previously noted small soft tissue residual at the left level IIA nodal station has completely resolved — that region is no longer identifiable as a distinct structure. Mild radiation-related soft tissue changes in the anterior and lateral neck soft tissues are stable. No new cervical lymphadenopathy. No evidence of radiation-induced osteonecrosis of the mandible. Carotid arteries with stable mild atherosclerotic intimal changes, no significant interval progression, consistent with the carotid duplex findings. Impression per radiology: Stable post-treatment changes; no evidence of recurrent or new malignancy at 24 months post-treatment.

(Note: CT chest at 18 months was negative for pulmonary or mediastinal disease. No new chest imaging ordered today per protocol; will obtain at 30 months as part of ongoing surveillance.)

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0 — STATUS POST DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CHEMORADIOTHERAPY (CISPLATIN + IMRT 70 GY)
   -- Complete response maintained at 2-year mark. NED confirmed by MRI neck today and clinical exam including nasopharyngoscopy. This is an excellent outcome entirely consistent with the favorable biology of HPV-positive, p16-strongly-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma in a never-smoker with good performance status.
   -- Risk of recurrence is low and decreasing. The vast majority of HPV+ oropharyngeal carcinoma recurrences, when they occur, happen within the first 2 years. Reaching 2 years NED is highly reassuring. I discussed this with the patient in detail. She expressed relief and gratitude.
   -- Surveillance plan going forward: Transition to every 6-month clinical visits (years 3-5 per NCCN guidelines). Annual MRI neck to continue. CT chest at 30 months (approximately 6 months from now). PET-CT not routinely planned unless clinical or imaging concern arises. Annual TSH check. Carotid duplex per vascular surgery schedule.
   -- No adjuvant therapy indicated or planned.

2. LATE EFFECTS OF CHEMORADIOTHERAPY
   a. Xerostomia: Grade 1, well-managed. Patient is on pilocarpine 5 mg TID and uses artificial saliva spray PRN as well as frequent water intake. She finds this manageable and does not want to escalate therapy. No changes to regimen at this time. Will continue to monitor; may consider dose adjustment if symptoms worsen.
   b. Dysgeusia: Grade 1. Partial recovery; approximately 80% of pre-treatment taste function per patient report. This degree of recovery at 2 years is typical and further improvement is possible, though likely to plateau. Reassurance provided; no specific intervention.
   c. Dysphagia: Mild, grade 1. Tolerating regular diet with thin liquids. Occasional difficulty with very dry or rough-textured foods. No aspiration events or pneumonia. Formal swallowing therapy has been discontinued. Patient performing home exercises independently. Will refer back to speech pathology if symptoms worsen.
   d. Trismus: Resolved. Mouth opening 41 mm (baseline 42 mm). No further intervention required.
   e. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism: Grade 2 at time of diagnosis (approximately 10 months post-treatment), now well-controlled on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. TSH 1.7 mIU/L today. Continue current levothyroxine dose; annual TSH check.
   f. Ototoxicity: Grade 1 bilateral high-frequency threshold shift at 4 kHz documented on audiogram at 18 months. Patient denies functional hearing impairment in daily life. No hearing aids required. Will repeat audiogram at 3-year mark or sooner if patient notes symptomatic change.
   g. Carotid atherosclerosis/radiation vasculopathy: Mild bilateral intimal-medial thickening, no hemodynamically significant stenosis. Under surveillance by vascular surgery with annual carotid duplex. Counseled patient on symptoms of cerebrovascular events; encouraged cardiovascular risk factor modification (diet, exercise). Already a non-smoker and non-drinker — optimal baseline.
   h. Dental: Fluoride rinse ongoing; routine dental visits maintained. No dental procedures requiring pre-procedural hyperbaric oxygen at this time. She was counseled to always inform her dentist of her prior head and neck radiation and to avoid invasive extractions in the radiation field without oncology consultation.
   i. Radiation-induced skin changes: Stable mild hyperpigmentation of bilateral neck — no intervention needed.
   j. No osteoradionecrosis. No soft tissue necrosis.

3. SURVIVORSHIP AND QUALITY OF LIFE
   -- Patient has fully resumed professional activities as high school principal. ECOG PS 0.
   -- Quality of life is excellent by patient's report. She finds the minor residual xerostomia and taste alteration to be acceptable given the trade-off of being cured of her cancer.
   -- She has been an active participant in a head and neck cancer survivorship support group and reports this to be a significant source of support and meaning.
   -- Psychosocial screening: No evidence of depression or anxiety at today's visit. No further referral indicated at this time.
   -- HPV vaccination counseled: Discussed importance of HPV vaccination series completion in her adolescent children. She confirmed both children have completed the series.
   -- Survivorship care plan: Updated and provided to patient today. Copies forwarded to primary care physician and endocrinology. Patient encouraged to maintain primary care follow-up for cardiovascular risk management and general preventive care.

4. ONGOING MEDICATIONS (relevant oncologic and late effects)
   -- Levothyroxine 100 mcg PO daily (radiation-induced hypothyroidism)
   -- Pilocarpine 5 mg PO TID (xerostomia)
   -- Fluoride dental rinse daily
   -- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily (initiated per vascular surgery recommendation for carotid changes; confirmed today)

Return to clinic in approximately 6 months for next surveillance visit. MRI neck to be ordered at that time in conjunction with visit. Interval: patient to call with any new neck mass, throat pain, dysphagia, hoarseness, ear pain, or other concerning symptoms. After-visit summary and updated survivorship care plan provided.

Total visit time: 50 minutes, greater than 50% counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed — Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Survivorship]
